title: Deodorant on Cotton
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (aluminum salts + oil + wax). Deodorant bonds to cotton (cellulose) through aluminum compounds that react with sweat proteins; wax base bonds to fiber. Treatment strategy: multi-phase approach addressing each component separately — dry-side for wax/oil first, then wet-side for the mineral/protein complex.
whyThisWorks: Deodorant is a combination stain — aluminum salts + oil + wax — each bonding differently. The wax and oil must be removed with a dry-side solvent before the water-based treatment can reach the aluminum-protein complex underneath.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG to address the oil/wax component. Tamp gently. Flush with VDS. This must be done BEFORE any wet-side treatment. Dwell 3–5 minutes.

2. After oil removed, apply Tannin Formula or Protein Formula based on dominant stain component — Protein Formula for body-soil/antiperspirant buildup, Tannin Formula if oxidized yellowing is present. Tamp and flush. Follow with H₂O₂ 6% if discoloration remains. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
3. Flush thoroughly with NSD on spotting board. Apply leveling agent to feathered edges. Extract with wet vac. Neutralize pH if acid or alkali was used.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — NEVER use hot water on deodorant stains; heat sets the aluminum-protein complex
warnings: Rubbing instead of blotting — rubbing pushes the stain deeper into cotton fibers
Applying heat before stain is fully removed — heat sets the aluminum-protein complex permanently

steps: 1. Remove any excess deodorant by blotting with a clean white cloth. Press firmly and lift straight up — never rub. Work from the outside edges toward the center.
2. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch generously over the stain. Let it sit for 10–15 minutes.
3. Brush off the powder. Apply a drop of Dawn dish soap directly to the stain and work it in gently with your fingertip.
4. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Blot dry with a clean towel.
5. Air dry completely. Do NOT use heat until you are certain the stain is completely gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If two home treatments haven't removed the deodorant, a professional cleaner with commercial-grade chemistry can often finish the job.
warnings: NEVER use hot water on deodorant stains — heat sets aluminum-protein complex.
Test any cleaning solution on a hidden area first.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Apply hot water or heat — sets the aluminum-protein complex permanently
Rub instead of blot — pushes stain deeper into cotton fibers
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is chemically durable. Standard spotting chemistry is safe. Always test on hidden area for colorfastness on dyed goods.

escalation.when: Two professional-grade treatments haven't resolved the stain, or stain has permanently bonded
escalation.whatToTell: Deodorant stain on cotton, combination of aluminum salts and wax/oil. Age unknown. Treated at home with dish soap. Please use sequential dry-side then wet-side protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this a fresh deodorant smear or a long-term buildup (pit stain)?
Is the fabric white or colored?
What type of deodorant — solid stick, gel, spray, antiperspirant?
Has the garment been through the dryer with the stain still present?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Long-term pit stain buildup is much harder to remove than fresh deodorant smears. Antiperspirant (with aluminum) creates the yellowing reaction with sweat proteins. Dryer exposure sets the stain significantly. Gels and sprays penetrate differently than solid sticks.
